transcript.text: Hi, Valentina, nice to meet you.
Hello, Bridget.
Nice to meet you, too.
Where are you based?
Oh, yeah.
So, yeah.
First of all, I wanted to say that this doesn't look professional at all.
And I'm sorry, I am on vacations right now.
Oh, my gosh.
Thank you for doing this.
I didn't even notice.
You could be in an office right now.
Oh, no, no.
So this is Baru.
It's an island 20 minutes away from Cartagena.
Do you know Cartagena?
Have you heard of it?
Yeah.
I'm there.
So I'm in a celebration.
Yeah.
I'm sorry.
Where do you normally live?
Thank you.
Oh, I've been there.
You've been to Pereira?
Yeah.
That's surprising.
That is very, very surprising.
Why were you?
Well, I was in Colombia for a month, and so we were in Armenia for a while, but I flew out.
I was, like, in the airport.
I don't know.
I think I had dinner in Pereira, and then I flew out of there back to Bogota because you have an airport.
Yeah, we have the airport.
Armenia doesn't have any airports yet.
Okay, so that's what I was.
Oh, yeah.
So, yeah, you've been to my city.
That's where I live.
Cool.
I usually live in Canada.
I'm in San Francisco today, or this week, with our teams here.
Oh, that's nice.
Yeah.
I've never been to Canada, but I've been to the U.S., but not to Canada.
Nice.
I think it's pretty cold.
Yeah, it is.
It's not like Colombia at all.
No.
Well, thank you for doing this on your vacation.
I'll try to let you know.
No, Thank
But I was, I watched your, the Hire Flix interview, and I was looking at your resume.
So yeah, I can tell you a little bit about us and like our company and what we're looking for, and then would love to like dig into more about like you and what you're interested in and all that.
Um, so, so we're essentially today we operate like a growth marketing agency.
So we, like our primary kind of service and product is that we produce a lot of different content.
So like blog posts, long form stuff on people's websites, essentially, and we also help with like SEO and GEO, which means like showing up in AI search now is like the big craze.
Like how do you, you know, when people are asking products, how do you get it to show up?
So different types of content that helps you get noticed by the LLMs.
Um, so we have like 50 ish customers today.
Um, they all have pretty high spend with us.
So it's like a very high touch model.
And so, yeah, we have a team.
Our team's about 70 people right now all over the world.
Definitely a lot of people in the U.S., like probably 25 or 30 are in the U.S., but everybody else is in like we're in 22 countries.
So it's super international.
And yeah, this role I'm looking for is, it's very, it's going to be super broad, the scope.
Like, I think there's going to be like, depending on where exactly you fall and like people have been moving around.
So that's another thing.

But like, there will be some, depending on who you like are supporting primarily, there will be some like traditional EA responsibilities, like helping with, you know, scheduling an email and all that.
But I don't think that will be at all like the majority of the work.
A lot of what we need is just different.
So sometimes it's like, we need help with client work, we need help generating more content.
So we need somebody to like collect all these different artifacts for the customer.
There's times when we need help with like, we're throwing events, and we need to get lists together, and we need to track who's attending and all that.
But another thing is like, we have some services where we like, use a broker, it's called backlinking, you like in the articles, you want to link to a lot of different things.
I do know what that is.
Okay, so we use like brokers for that.
So we need to be like, on top of it, like, are we doing it?
we delivering, tracking it, make sure the clients know the broker, everybody's on the same page.
So there's like different aspects of project management here.
And a lot of it's, I'm sure there will be some things that are like, ongoing over time.
But in general, it's a lot more just like, what's the what's happening this week?
And where can we use help?

And so like, I think the primary people you would be working with will be, it will be between, so our head of growth, who runs like our own marketing and growth, like he definitely needs some more support.
And then our CEO, like he has, he currently has an EA who's doing a lot of like calendar management and emailing his Slack and his LinkedIn is crazy because he has 20,000 followers on LinkedIn.
Um, so she's responding to people.
So there's that, but then there's this like other element for him and his chief of staff need help with just like special projects and client work.
Like they, they themselves manage a lot of clients.
Um, so being like more, helping them more with like, um, projects and client stuff as well.
So, um, I don't know, I, I say this like, yes, there, there could be, and there probably will be some more traditional EA responsibilities, but in general, super cool
I think of it as more of like helping with everything and anything kind of.

So I'll pause there because I'm curious, like I know you've done like lots of EA stuff as well, but I'm really mostly curious to learn more about outside of that, like what kind of projects you've done, what's interesting to you, like what are some ways you've tried to like level yourself up and things like that?
Okay, so to be quite honest, I've never been in EA.
Anyway, I've been a growth manager, growth specialist, and then process improver.
Okay, I thought I saw like management of like, well, maybe not.

No, I think you've read management in the, I graduated three years ago from a business school, European business school, and with a focus in management, but to be honest with you, I was, and that's basically my, that's me.
I actually specialize in three different areas, which is finance.
Okay.
Marketing and management.
that basically defines me as a person, which is a little bit of this, a little bit of that, a little bit.
And that also aligns with what you're saying that I need to do, which is a little bit of this, a little bit of that.
And that's what I like to do.
I like to see where I can be helpful.
And if it's with clients, that's, that works because I also, I also feel that in order for you to get to be helpful, you need to know who are the clients of the company, what, what they need.
And you actually need to have conversations with them or at least have this feeling with them.
Yeah.
Like what's the purpose and like, what are the greater goals?
Yeah.
So the, to, and, and to build trust.
Um, I believe that that's very, very, important for a company to grow is, is trust.
You need clients to trust you, to trust your team.
And so, um, uh, I get, I get that.
And I feel like I can do all of that.
All of what you said is what I've been doing for the past two years, basically, which is improving processes.
Okay.
So that's like, so what type of company is this current company?
The last company I worked for, it was a healthcare, it was a plastic surgery clinic in Pereira.
And when I got there, they didn't have, they didn't even know where they were standing.
So the way I see, what I do is, is just get to a place and I see, okay, how is, are we, do we have a structure for growth?
Because in the past, I've worked for startups where growth was a little bit chaotic.
So it didn't have no heads and toes.
It was just like all over it.
And then I, when I switched to internal growth, because at the
First, I was more focused on external growth.
So that's why I know a little bit of what you're talking about, which is like the linking and a little bit of what you do, which is content marketing.
I know a little bit of that because I've been around that at the beginning of my career.
I was basically doing that with the teams that were in charge of that.
But two years ago, we switched to internal growth, which is I was more interested in how can I help build this structure if the company doesn't have it?
And if it has it, then how can I improve it?
How can we make things better?
That's what I do.
What's like an example of some of the things you've done there?
Okay, so first I created a database because when I got there, they didn't have data.
They didn't know how.
How much money they were making.
It was sort of what happens to small, medium-sized business in Colombia.
They're very, like the owners are like very, I don't know how to say that, but they run the business just like the way they can.
They do not have a structure.
So when I got there, that's what I did.
I created a database with Google, in Google.
So I created some folders.
And I automated the way they would track the surgery, like the indumentary, or I don't know how to say that in English, actually.
The things that they would use in surgery, it was by hand.
So a paper, they would.
inventory, yeah.
Yeah.
So in each surgery, the doctors would use some, like some tools from the inventory, right?
They would write it down in some special papers.
Like,
And then management would receive that, take that, and put it by hand in an Excel sheet.
But the Excel sheet wasn't connected, for example, to the inventory, to the real inventory.
So imagine they would take that, then that paper would go to pharmacy, and from pharmacy they would also take out everything that they've used by hand.
And the Excel wasn't connected with the prices also, so management had, like, they never had the prices updated, like the cost of the things updated.
So it was chaotic, it was bad, but I then linked everything, so the process was then online.
Right now, in the surgery, they do it in a Google sheet special thing that I created.
They send that to management and to pharmacy, and then pharmacy.
They can discharge that and automatically link, they link that to the inventory.
So that's done automatically right now.
And management, they are now connected to pharmacy.
So they have, first of all, the cost of the products are updated.
So they know if they're overcharging, like they're charging way too much for a product, or if they're losing, like they're charging less than what the cost of the product is.
And then they also, the sheet that I created, it basically can compute the final, what the surgeon owes to the clinic.
It does automatically, they don't have to do it.
Because they will do it by hand.
So it was, yeah, I can actually, well, probably if you like a demonstration, I have a, like, I could ask for a demo or something like that, that I can create.
And then I can show you, like, what I mean, like, what I did with the purpose.
right now.
But what, when you say it's automated, how does it,
How does that work?
Like, is it syncing some other tool or something?
So Google has its own, like, code that you can put inside.
So every Google Sheet workbook has something called Apps Script, and it's JavaScript.
And you can basically create tables from zero.
You can do a lot of stuff.
So I can actually check my email, like, tell Google Apps Script to check my email and then create an Excel sheet in whatever book I want.
Like, I connect that with Apps Script, with JavaScript.
So did you learn how to do that?
I used AI.
So I didn't know how to do it because I am not a coder.
But I knew that it could be possible.
So when I looked for it, I was like, okay, I'm going to try, and it worked.
So I did everything there.
Wow.
Oh, and what, okay, so what's this company before, yeah, tell me about the two companies before that, RocketFi and SimpleBI.
What did they do and what did you do there?
So SimpleBI is a company that, it's a staffing company, actually, SimpleBI.
It's a staffing company that basically does data science or business analytics for other medium and big companies.
And so it's, what they do is they, the company needs, for example, support in their data science area or in their business intelligence area.
And they would basically hire SimpleBI to do so.
And SimpleBI would have, actually, was quite small, like three, four other developers, and they would.
Send, like, assign a developer to a company or a project, and it would be project-based.
And what I did there was try to do two things.
First of all, for the actual client, the clients that they had before I came in to retain them.
Like, how can we retain them?
Can we improve the ticket spending and all of that?
And then, also, how can we acquire new clients?
And that was quite hard.
It was very, very niche.
Very.
It was a hard one.
What were some of the, like, things that worked that you tried?
So, it was, we did, like, a cold email outreach with LinkedIn.
And that's how we got one client.
I mean, it was, it was, sorry, it was, was, I mean, for me, it was successful.
Because I've never done a LinkedIn outreach campaign before.
But it was also, I get that there are two things that I know now.
First of all, I was not a content writing expert or like an outreach expert.
And that's, I think it's needed for companies to grow, to actually hire people that are experts in like those certain areas.
And the second thing is, it was very niche.
But yeah, with them, with the current clients, we started to do like a feedback, testimonies kind of idea.
So they were to post it in the website to attract more clients.
I actually helped build the website that they have right now.
It was more of that type of thing.
And what about retention?
But that was another aspect.
Like what kind of strategies was there to try to retain the customer?
It's kind of project-based, so I guess it's a bit different.
Yeah, it was a bit different.
So what we did was sometimes it was hard when the client didn't hire us directly.
Why?
Because we were hired through a bigger staffing company.
So it was a big staffing company than Simple BI, which was another staffing company, specializing in Simple BI.

what we were trying to actually do, it was because of the contractual, it was contractually, we couldn't ask the client directly to like, if they would like to stay with us for another project, if they needed something, et cetera.
But we did with the, we started working like more closely with the, because at first we tried,
This is something that also I agree that we actually do is when something is working, I think we should double down on that.
And we tried to skip the middleman just because the middleman was keeping a lot of money and there were no ones working, right?
But at the end, the strategy that was working is keeping the middleman, having better relationship with them so they can find us more clients and then just continue working that way.
Because that big client, for example, Microsoft was one of the big clients.
They will hire middlemen because they know middlemen and middlemen will hire us.
But if you try to do the connection yourself, they're just going to ignore you.
That's going to be too hard.
Okay.
Yeah.
And then what about this company before called RocketFi?
What does that do?
It is still.
It is an e-commerce company.
It is a Colombian startup and they are...
They are a platform to connect sellers with buyers.
So they help you with the logistics.
If you're an e-commerce, for example, I sell, I don't know, sportswear.
They will allow you to have your website there and they will do the logistics.
So if someone buys from you, they will organize all the logistics, the packaging with the, yeah.
So we will be like also, it will be like a service connecting different users.
And our clients will be the company selling because we would keep a percentage of their sales.
So that's how we would, yeah.
And you were already there for four months.
How come it was so short?
Yeah, I quit.
Three months later.
I actually worked for three months.
They would give us their three months of training, you can call it that, to see if they want to keep you, and then they renew your contract.
And when I was going to renew, I decided that I didn't want to do it.
Why?
Because it was crazy growth.
It was not organized, and it was very chaotic, and I just didn't like the company, working for the company.
The company is great, but I didn't like working for the company.
If you could have changed something, what would you have changed?
I would say that people on the inside, they thought you were competition.
So that was hard.
As soon as I got in, the idea is for me to go and help.
The different growth things that already existed and help them structure their processes and, for example, do email.
For example, if I see that the email sequence for clients is not that good, then I should go and work with a team to make it better.
And the issue with that is that because people thought that I was coming for their jobs, I don't know why, because I had my own position.
It was very hard to have that sort of trust and people would like, if you want to do it, just go and do it.
But I am not the expert in that area.
So, yeah, it was difficult to try and make some changes.
They moved me to lead something called communities because no one was having results there.
So, they were like, okay, you're going to lead communities.
And they basically put me after like a month to lead a completely new team in a different, in growth.
don't know.
yeah.
Yeah.
Like in a different position that I was supposed to be doing.
was chaotic and I just didn't feel good.
I didn't feel good because I didn't feel like, I didn't even know what I was doing.
Like, am I doing something good?
I had just graduated.
I was a little bit lost.
Yeah.
I see.
great.
What are some things like you've taught yourself recently, I guess?
It could be for work or outside of work, but I'm just curious.
Like, are some things you've been trying to learn recently?
I've tried, but I haven't.
I tried to learn N8N, you know, the thing that automates like things.
But at the end, I gave it.
Because I actually wanted to implement N8N to improve like the chat so that the receptionist wouldn't have to deal with all of that and to improve communication.
But to be honest, then I gave up.
Because I realise...
It was going to take me like some time and they didn't need me.
The secretary was doing just her work and I was like, yeah, no, but I wanted to learn N8N to improve customer service.
That's what I wanted to focus on.
how would it work?
Like someone chat, is it a chat on the website?
No, N8N is more like, do you know?
Oh, I know N8N, but how were you, how were you picturing N8N helping with the?
No, no, no.
I would connect directly through WhatsApp, the API.
That was also something what I didn't do because it would cost them money.
And I didn't see it.
Honestly, I didn't see that that was the business to, to where, where else to implement it.
What would you, what did you have in mind?
Like someone is WhatsAppping for support and then what happened, what needs to happen?
So the people.
The idea would be for the chatbot to actually own the conversations until it needs a human being.
So for it to learn and understand when it needs human interaction, for example, with some, like when the person specifically asks for like a human or something like that, they will pass it to Diana.
So the idea was it would handle everything until a human was actually needed.
Got it.
But here it's quite difficult because the patients they like to call a lot and also the ones that just had surgery, like they would need human interaction right away.
So it was going to be very, very complicated and then not worth it.
Yeah, I could see like, especially in healthcare.
And it's a, it's elective and it's expensive.
So it's like, expect.
no.
Yeah.
Yeah, it wasn't, it wasn't, it wasn't a good idea.
To be honest, I don't see how it would actually add value there.
It would only cost money in that case, in that particular case.
Yeah, but it's cool for other things, like just making some workflows so that you could, I don't know, when you...
I would say for, yeah, no, but the thing is the workflows are done through Apps Script.
I didn't need, if I hadn't done that.
At first, I said, like, should I, like, transfer all my flows to N810?
That was something I asked myself.
But to be honest, everything is already connected.
And it doesn't add anything.
It actually cost me the server because I was going to deploy it locally.
And then it was just, they will have to pay for the server.
Apps Script is free.
I actually connected even Google Apps Script to Telegram.
Telegram sends the updates.
So,
In the reception, the receptionist, she updates an Excel table with, for example, next week's procedures.
And she used to, like, every time she updated something, she would take a photo of that and she would send it to a WhatsApp group, because in Colombia we use WhatsApp.
She would send it to WhatsApp with the, basically, the scheduled surgeries for the team to be aware of, okay, we have this.
Then I connected a Google Sheet table to Telegram, and now every time she updates, it waits for an hour, because sometimes she makes a lot of updates.
It doesn't even make sense to, like, send 20 updates in a, yeah.
So it waits for an hour, it understands what has been changed, and then it sends the updates.
And every Saturday at 10 a.m., it also sends the schedule for the next.
Next week and things like that.
So there's things that I have improved for in different areas.
So they don't have to be like doing this small thing here, small thing here, small thing here.
Awesome.
I have to go because I have another meeting, but I don't know if it's possible that you could send me like a little video or something of some of the stuff you've built.
I can.
Okay.
Let's do that.
And then I know you're on vacation.
So I don't want, I want you to enjoy that, but send me a video.
And then I'm traveling back home tonight and tomorrow.
So probably like after you send me that, maybe we can be in touch on the next steps after that for sure.
Could I send you the videos to your email?
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's perfect.
Okay.
Okay.
And can I send you the videos tonight?
Yeah.
No rush.
Like today, tomorrow, next week, whatever works for you.
know you're on vacation.
No, no, no.
I want to send, I want to send it.
I actually, when I received that, when I read to the, I know you have to go, but.
When I read the job offer, I was so, I was surprised because there is no executive assistant or other position that actually matches my profile as good as this one.
It's so weird.
I'm like, wait, this is what I do.
Yeah, when I watched your video and everything, because, yeah, I was like, oh, wow, this is a cool fit.
So, yeah, send me that stuff.

I love to see that because, like, what makes the difference here between someone, you know, like our, like, strongest people in the company is, like, you they're, they're, like, teaching themselves things, they're thinking about how to be more efficient, they're, like, building little bits of leverage everywhere, and that's their mind.
That's, like, their first thought is, like, how can I be more productive and efficient and, like, not waiting for someone to tell them what to do or, you know, how to do it.
It's like, oh, I saw this inefficient thing and I already have a solution that I came up with, you know, so it's that mindset.
Cool.
Well, so nice to meet you.
I look forward to seeing your video.
We'll chat soon.
Yeah, yeah.
Thank you, Bridget.
Okay, bye.
Have a nice day.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Introductory interview between Bridget McGillivray and Valentina Giraldo Morales for a broad-scope growth/operations role at Bridget's content marketing agency.](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=381.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role involves diverse responsibilities: client work, content generation, event planning, project management, and some EA duties](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=563.0)
  - [Valentina's background in growth, process improvement, and data automation aligns well with the position's needs](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=745.0)
  - [Valentina demonstrated initiative in learning new skills (e.g., Google Apps Script) to automate processes at previous jobs](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1183.0)
  - [Both parties expressed mutual interest in moving forward with next steps](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=2099.0)

## Topics

### Company Overview

  - [Growth marketing agency producing various content (blog posts, SEO/GEO optimization)](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=496.0)
  - [\~50 high-spend customers, high-touch model](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=533.0)
  - [Team of \~70 people across 22 countries (25-30 in US)](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=551.0)

### Role Description

  - [Broad scope, adaptable to weekly needs](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=563.0)
  - [Mix of traditional EA duties and project-based work](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=581.0)
  - [Potential responsibilities:](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=596.0)
      - [Client work and content generation](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=600.0)
      - [Event planning and attendee tracking](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=611.0)
      - [Project management (e.g., backlinking services)](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=635.0)
      - [Supporting head of growth and CEO](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
      - [Special projects and client management assistance](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=692.0)

### Valentina's Experience

  - [Recent focus: internal growth and process improvement](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=844.0)
  - [Created database and automated inventory tracking for healthcare clinic](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=950.0)
      - [Used Google Sheets and Apps Script to link surgery inventory, pharmacy, and management](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1145.0)
      - [Improved cost tracking and billing processes](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1106.0)
  - [Previous roles in growth management at SimpleBI (staffing) and RocketFi (e-commerce)](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1214.0)
      - [Implemented client retention and acquisition strategies](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1275.0)
      - [Faced challenges with rapid, unstructured growth at RocketFi](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1582.0)

### Skills and Initiatives

  - [Self-taught Google Apps Script for process automation](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1183.0)
  - [Attempted to learn N8N for customer service improvement (unrealized)](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1799.0)
  - [Connected Google Sheets to Telegram for automated surgery schedule updates](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1913.0)
  - [Demonstrated ability to identify inefficiencies and implement tech solutions](https://fathom.video/share/UHhMGRBv4haK2jLpMtLScyikDw6XRQAC?tab=summary&timestamp=1950.0)
